title: Image Augmentation
concept: Image augmentation applies label-preserving visual changes like flips, crops, rotations, and color shifts so a vision model learns the object matters more than the exact camera setup.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What makes an augmented example useful?

options.text: The example gains variety while keeping the same important label.
The correct answer changes every time.

exercises.explain: Correct. Augmentation helps only when the model is seeing harmless variation, not a changed label.

cards.title: The object is the same even when the camera moves
cards.body: A dog is still a dog if the photo is slightly rotated, cropped, mirrored, or brightened. Image augmentation teaches the network to focus less on one perfect camera setup and more on the object itself.

cards.title: Teaching visual invariance
cards.body: Image augmentation uses changes like flips, crops, rotations, and color shifts to teach a model some visual invariance. That means the prediction should stay stable even when the picture changes in harmless ways.

cards.title: Recognizing a friend in different outfits
cards.body: You still recognize your friend if they wear a hat, stand a little to the left, or walk under warmer lighting. A vision model needs that same flexibility. Otherwise it mistakes camera conditions for the real identity of the object.

cards.title: A sick leaf at many angles
cards.body: Suppose a plant-disease model only sees straight, centered leaf photos taken at noon. A few safe rotations, crops, and brightness shifts can teach it that the disease pattern matters more than the exact angle or sunlight level.

cards.title: More distortion is not always better
cards.body: A common myth is that the strongest possible image edits always help most. But if you rotate too far, crop away the object, or change colors unrealistically, you may create training examples that no longer match the real task.

cards.title: Pixel memorization vs. object invariance
cards.body: Good image augmentation nudges the model toward the object and away from the exact photograph.

left.text:
Centered object, familiar lighting, familiar framing.
Small camera changes can confuse the model.

right.text:
Object stays recognizable across flips, crops, rotations, and color shifts.
Predictions become steadier.

exercises.prompt: Why use flips, crops, or rotations during vision training?

options.text: To help the model learn that the object matters more than one exact framing or angle.
To erase the labels from the dataset.
To make every class use the same background color.
To guarantee the network never needs validation.

exercises.explain: Exactly. The goal is to make the prediction more stable across harmless visual changes.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Image augmentation is useful because many objects should keep the same label even if the photo is slightly rotated or brightened.
exercises.explain: True. Those small changes often preserve the object identity, so they are good ways to teach visual robustness.

exercises.sentence: Image augmentation often teaches the model visual ___.
exercises.explain: Invariance is correct. The model should keep its answer stable across small, harmless visual changes.

exercises.prompt: Which training plan sounds most sensible for image augmentation?

options.text: Use mild rotations, crops, and color shifts that still leave the object clearly visible.
Crop away most of the object so the model can guess from random background pixels.
Change labels after every flip so the dataset feels more diverse.

exercises.explain: Right. The transformations should reflect plausible camera variation while preserving the object label.

exercises.prompt: A road-sign model was trained mostly on centered daytime images. On real streets it struggles when signs appear slightly tilted, partly cropped, or under different lighting. What is the best fix conceptually?

options.text: Use image augmentation such as safe rotations, crops, and brightness changes so the model learns the sign should be recognized across those variations.
Train only on the original centered daytime photos so the model stays pure.
Replace all labels with “unknown” whenever the lighting changes.
Delete the hidden layers because camera angle is not important.

exercises.explain: Exactly. The failure pattern says the model has tied the label too closely to one visual setup instead of the underlying object.
